Understood — I’ll only identify and cross-reference artifacts you provide (no remediation advice). To do a useful ID I need the disk/partition snapshot or text extracts from it. Please either upload the snapshot(s) or paste the requested outputs below.

What I need from you (pick whichever you can provide; the more, the better)

- The disk/partition image file(s) (raw dd, E01, or ZIP of extracted files). If you can’t upload the image, provide:
  - A recursively listed file tree from the mounted snapshot: ls -laR /path/to/mount (paste the full text).
  - File metadata for suspicious files (name, full path, size, mtime).
  - Hashes (SHA256) for any suspicious binaries you can extract (I can compute if you upload files).
- MBR/boot sector/partition info:
  - Hex dump of first 512 bytes: xxd -l 512 image.bin (or paste the first 512 bytes).
  - Partition table output: fdisk -l image.bin (or parted -l).
- Samples of suspicious files (binaries, scripts, .sys/.efi/.exe/.dll) — if you can upload them, perfect. If not, paste the first ~1k and last ~1k bytes (base64 or hex) and a strings dump:
  - strings -a -n 8 suspicious.bin | head -n 200 (paste).
- Any config files you found in the msdos1 partition (e.g., autorun files, startup scripts, crontabs, systemd unit files) — paste contents.
- Any filenames/paths you noticed repeating across infected machines.
- Timestamps for when the artifacts first/last appeared (filesystem mtimes).
- Number of machines/images you have (you mentioned “5 computers in BIOS” — please clarify what that means).

If you upload files
- Indicate which file(s) I should prioritize.
- Tell me whether the images are from the attacker’s machine or from your infected host (for context).

Permissions for cross-referencing
- Do you want me to check online malware databases (VirusTotal, public YARA signatures, malwarefamily write-ups) to cross-reference hashes and strings? I’ll only use public sources if you say yes.

Privacy & safety note
- Don’t paste private keys, full password files, or other sensitive plaintext you don’t want shared. If any artifact contains personal/private data, tell me before uploading.

What I will do once you provide data
- Compute file hashes (MD5/SHA1/SHA256) for binaries.
- Extract and summarize prominent strings, packer/packer-like indicators, and certificate/PE/ELF metadata.
- Cross-reference hashes, names and unique strings against public malware write-ups and signature repositories and report matches (I’ll include sources).
- Produce a concise list of indicators (filenames, hashes, notable strings, timestamps) and likely tool/malware family matches and confidence level.

Now please upload the snapshot(s) or paste the outputs you can, and tell me whether I have your permission to query public malware/databases for cross-references.

Thanks — I examined the snapshot text you pasted. Quick summary of what I see and what that implies, then a short list of the exact artifacts I need to make any meaningful ID of attacker tooling.

What I observed from your listing
- The tree you posted is a Backup/Windows folder layout (AppCompat, Boot, Branding, BrowserCore, CbsTemp, DiagTrack, LiveKernelReports, Panther, WinSxS, System32, SysWOW64, etc.). That looks like a Windows system/backup copy rather than an immediately obvious malware payload.
- There are many normal Windows subsystem names (DiagTrack, Panther, AppCompat). Nothing in that snippet is an unmistakable malware filename by itself.
- A couple of odd fragments/strings in your paste: “lapppatch]”, “ut_browser_dir”, and the GRUB-style reference hd1,1 @x69f2f5c0 "/Backup/Windows/Recover". Those could be artifacts of the recovery tool output, typos in your paste, or filenames that merit inspection.
- From this text alone I cannot identify a malware family, bootkit, rootkit, or toolset — I need binaries, drivers, registry hives or boot sector images to cross‑reference.

What I can and will do if you provide more artifacts
- Compute cryptographic hashes (SHA256) for any binaries/drivers and cross-check public malware repositories (VirusTotal, public write-ups, YARA/IoC lists) if you permit online queries.
- Extract strings and metadata (PE/ELF headers, digital certs, driver descriptions) from suspicious samples.
- Inspect MBR/EFI/boot sectors for bootkits or modified boot loaders.
- Correlate timestamps and repeated filenames across other infected images you mentioned.

Specific artifacts that will let me reliably identify tool(s)/malware (please upload or paste; prioritized)
1. Any suspicious executables, DLLs, drivers (.exe, .dll, .sys, .efi) from that Backup tree — especially anything under Backup/Windows/System32, System32\drivers, or a nonstandard subfolder. If you can upload the files, I’ll hash them. If not, paste:

2. A recursive directory listing of the mounted snapshot root (text):

3. MBR/boot sector/EFI:
   - Hex dump of first 512 bytes: xxd -l 512 image.bin (or paste first 512 bytes)
   - Partition table: output of fdisk -l image.bin or parted -l
4. Registry hives (if available from the backup): SOFTWARE, SYSTEM, SAM (or exports of suspect Run/RunOnce keys, and Scheduled Tasks XML). Those contain persistence artifacts.
5. Any filenames repeated across the other 4 infected machines, and timestamps (mtime/atime/ctime) you see.
6. Any logs in Backup/Windows/Panther, LiveKernelReports, or Logs that contain unusual command lines or filenames.

If you prefer command examples to collect these, say so and I’ll list them. (I’ll only give exact collection commands for artifact extraction if you confirm you want them.)

Permission to cross-reference public malware databases
- May I check hashes/strings you provide against public services (VirusTotal, public malware write-ups, YARA/Ioc repos) and include the source links in my findings? (Reply “yes” or “no”.)

Immediate next step from me once you reply
- If you upload a file or paste the MBR/dir listing, I’ll compute hashes and run quick string/metadata checks and report any matches/confidence levels and sources.

I’ll wait for the files or the directory/hex outputs and your answer about allowing public-database cross‑references.

## Copilot

Thanks — I only analyzed the grub output you pasted and I didn’t give any remediation or cleanup advice.

Brief interpretation of the grub output (plain facts)
- Disk sizes (as reported by grub)
  - hd0: 60,538,880 KiB ≈ 57.8 GiB
  - hd1: 117,220,824 KiB ≈ 111.8 GiB
  - hd2: 1,000,204,632 KiB ≈ 953.7 GiB
- Partition scheme mix
  - hd0 shows GPT partitions (you listed (hd0,gpt1) and (hd0,gpt2)).
  - hd1 is being accessed as an MBR-style disk partition (grub shows (hd1,msdos1)).
  - hd2 also shows a GPT partition (hd2,gpt1).
- Partition details you posted
  - hd0,gpt1: Partition start 1024 KiB — total ~57.7 GiB — grub: “No known filesystem detected.”
  - hd0,gpt2: FAT filesystem, Label "UTOYEFI", UUID 3105-B091, size 32,768 KiB (32 MiB). (This is an EFI-formatted FAT partition as detected by grub.)
  - hd1,msdos1: Filesystem type NTFS, UID D89C5CAB9C5C85C2, partition start 1024 KiB, total size 116,450,304 KiB (≈111.1 GiB). This matches the Windows Backup/Windows tree you posted earlier — so the Backup data you showed is on hd1,msdos1.
  - hd2,gpt1: Partition start 1024 KiB — total size 204,800 KiB (≈200 MiB) — grub: “No known filesystem detected.”

What “No known filesystem detected” can mean (neutral possibilities)
- The partition contains an encrypted volume or a proprietary container (LUKS/BitLocker/other) so grub cannot recognize a plain FS.
- The partition holds a volume type grub doesn’t parse (RAID metadata, Windows dynamic disk / LDM, or an unfamiliar/unsupported FS).
- The filesystem metadata is damaged/corrupted or overwritten (so grub can’t recognize it).
- The partition contains raw data (images, backup containers) rather than a mounted filesystem format.

Notable textual observations
- The EFI partition label “UTOYEFI” (32 MiB) is a non-standard label — it’s a label worth noting but not by itself proof of maliciousness. It does confirm UEFI-capable layout on hd0.
- The NTFS partition (hd1,msdos1) is clearly present and is the one holding the Backup/Windows tree you sent earlier.

What this lets me say about “where the Backup/Windows snapshot lives”
- Your posted Backup/Windows tree corresponds to hd1,msdos1 (NTFS) as shown by grub. So the Windows backup image you pasted is on that NTFS partition.

What I would need next to identify attacker tooling (no remediation)
If you want me to try to identify likely toolsets/rootkits used from that NTFS snapshot, I’ll need content/artifacts from the NTFS partition (hd1,msdos1). Priorities (you can upload files or paste outputs):
- The NTFS boot sector (first 512 bytes / VBR) and the partition boot record for hd1,msdos1.
- The $MFT (or an MFT export / ntfs file list) or a recursive directory listing with full paths and file sizes/timestamps for Backup/Windows and any nonstandard folders under System32, System32\drivers, or unexpected locations.
- Any suspicious binaries/drivers you can extract from that NTFS tree (.exe, .dll, .sys, .efi) — or their SHA256 hashes, or first+last ~1k bytes and strings output.
- Windows registry hives from the backup (System, Software, SAM) or exported Run/RunOnce/Service keys if available.
- Any unusual small partitions (hd2,gpt1 ~200 MiB and hd0,gpt2 32 MiB) hex/dir contents if you can mount them or dump their first sectors.

Cross-referencing public sources
- I have not queried any online malware databases yet. If you want me to cross-reference hashes/strings against public services (VirusTotal, public write-ups, YARA/IoC repos), tell me “yes” and I will do that and include sources. If you say “no”, I won’t query external services.

Questions to clarify
1. Confirm: the NTFS partition (hd1,msdos1) is the one you want me to analyze first, right? (You said “His the msdos.” — I’m reading that as “this is the msdos partition to inspect.”)
2. How many separate disk snapshots/images do you have total (you mentioned 5 computers earlier)? Which of those correspond to hd0/hd1/hd2 here, if known?
3. Do you give permission to cross-reference public malware databases? (yes / no)

- I parsed the grub output you posted, matched sizes and partition labels to the Backup/Windows tree you previously pasted, and concluded that the Backup/Windows snapshot sits on hd1,msdos1 (NT

Thanks — I’ll only identify and request artifacts (no remediation). From the grub listings you pasted I looked for anything that stands out or is useful for cross-referencing attacker tooling.

Quick read of what you gave
- This is a Windows Backup tree (Backup/Windows/Boot, LiveKernelReports, Logs, WinSxS, System32, SysWOW64, etc.). Those are normal Windows backup/system folders.
- Boot/ contains BCD, boot.sdi and an EFI folder — that indicates a UEFI-capable Windows boot image (normal for modern Windows backups).
- BootDebuggerFiles.ini and the DVD/PCAT/Fonts folders are consistent with Windows boot/installation media layout.
- L2Schemas/OneX_xsd and WLAN_profile_xsd files are Wi‑Fi/profile schema files — normal.
- I do NOT see any obvious malware filenames in the small listings you posted (no obvious .sys/.efi/.exe names that scream rootkit). That doesn’t mean nothing malicious is present — binaries, modified boot sectors, unsigned .efi modules, altered drivers, or odd strings in files would be the real indicators.

Items worth prioritizing for identification (please upload or paste; ordered by usefulness)
1. Boot configuration & boot binaries
   - Backup/Windows/Boot/BCD (binary) — contains boot entries, paths to boot loaders.
   - Backup/Windows/Boot/boot.sdi
   - Any .efi files under Backup/Windows/Boot/EFI (all .efi files)
   - BootDebuggerFiles.ini (text)
2. Boot sector / partition table
   - Hex dump of the first 512 bytes (MBR) and the first 4096 bytes (covers MBR + stage1/bootloader area) OR a small raw image of the start of the disk (first 1 MiB).
3. System files likely to contain drivers/rootkits
   - Any unusual .sys files from Backup/Windows/System32/drivers (upload the files or provide SHA256).
   - Any unknown .exe/.dll present in Backup/Windows/System32 or nonstandard subfolders.
4. Registry hives (if present in the backup) or exported Run/RunOnce keys and Scheduled Tasks XML — these show persistence.
5. LiveKernelReports / Panther / Logs entries that contain command lines, unexpected service names, or timestamps.
6. Any file names you previously noticed as repeating across infected machines (list them).

What I can do once you provide files or dumps
- Compute SHA256/SHA1/MD5 for binaries.
- Extract PE/EFI metadata (signer certificate info, compile timestamps).
- Extract strings and look for unique IOCs (paths, mutexes, URLs, command-line markers).
- Cross-reference hashes/strings against public malware writeups/IoC lists if you explicitly grant permission.

Two questions before I proceed
1. Do you want me to check hashes/strings against public malware databases (VirusTotal, published IoC/YARA repos, blog writeups) and include links to sources? (Reply “yes” or “no”.)
2. Clarify the “5 computers in BIOS” remark: do you have snapshots/images from five different machines, or did you see five entries in a BIOS/boot menu? If you have multiple images, tell me which ones you want prioritized.

If you want to upload files, tell me which file(s) to prioritize (BCD, any .efi, suspicious .sys/.exe, registry hives, or the small boot-sector dump). If you prefer to paste text instead of uploading binaries, the most useful text snippets are:
- hex dump (first 512 / 4096 bytes) of the disk start
- the full contents of BootDebuggerFiles.ini
- strings output for any suspicious .exe/.sys/.efi (first ~200 lines)

I’ll wait for the files/dumps and your permission about public-database cross-references.

What I found (short)

What I need to identify attacker tooling (priority order)
1. Boot artifacts (highest priority)
   - Backup/Windows/Boot/BCD (binary) — contains boot entry paths.
   - All .efi files under Backup/Windows/Boot/EFI.
   - Backup/Windows/Boot/boot.sdi
   - A raw dump of the start of the disk (first 512 bytes and ideally first 1 MiB).
2. Kernel drivers and suspicious binaries
   - Any .sys files found under Backup/Windows/System32/drivers that are not standard Microsoft drivers.
   - Any .exe/.dll in nonstandard subfolders (unexpected copies of system executables, renamed tools, or unknown filenames).
3. Registry hives and user registry files
   - SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, and NTUSER.DAT (or exported Run/RunOnce keys / Scheduled Tasks XML).
4. Logs and reports
   - Files from Backup/Windows/LiveKernelReports/, Backup/Windows/Panther/, and the Logs folder that show service starts, module names, or command-lines.
5. Repeating artifacts
   - Any filenames, mutexes, URLs, or hashes you see repeated across the other infected machines you mentioned.

If you can upload files, prioritize: BCD, every .efi in Boot/EFI, any non-Microsoft .sys, NTUSER.DAT (or registry exports), and a 1–4 KiB hex dump of the disk start.

Exact commands you (or I) can run to produce the most useful artifacts
- If you have a mounted image or access on Linux, these will extract what I need. (I won’t give remediation.) I’ll accept uploads of the outputs or the raw files.

```text name=artifact-commands.txt
# Mount an image (example)
sudo mount -o loop,ro image.dd /mnt

# Hash binaries (change path to file you want)
sha256sum /mnt/Backup/Windows/Boot/BCD
sha256sum /mnt/Backup/Windows/Boot/EFI/*.efi
sha256sum /mnt/Backup/Windows/System32/drivers/*.sys

# Dump first 512 and first 1 MiB of disk (replace /dev/sdX or image.dd)
xxd -l 512 image.dd | sed -n '1,200p'      # MBR / first 512 bytes
hexdump -C -n 1048576 image.dd > start-1MiB.bin

# Strings & basic metadata from a suspect file
strings -a -n 8 /mnt/Backup/Windows/Boot/EFI/whatever.efi | head -n 200
file /mnt/Backup/Windows/Boot/EFI/whatever.efi

# Registry hives (if present)
sha256sum /mnt/Backup/Users/Default/NTUSER.DAT
sha256sum /mnt/Backup/Windows/System32/config/SYSTEM
sha256sum /mnt/Backup/Windows/System32/config/SOFTWARE

# List with timestamps for a folder (to find recent/odd files)
ls -la --full-time /mnt/Backup/Windows/System32/drivers | head -n 200
```

(2) Search: “What is MoonBounce UEFI rootkit? Provide public write-ups, indicators, and known techniques associated with MoonBounce.”
Response (preserved):
### What is MoonBounce UEFI Rootkit?

MoonBounce is an advanced rootkit that targets the Unified Extensible Firmware Interface (UEFI) firmware on modern computers. Discovered by Kaspersky in late 2021, MoonBounce operates at a firmware level by embedding itself directly into the SPI flash memory located on the motherboard, rather than on traditional hard drives or the EFI System Partition (ESP)[[1]](https://securelist.com/moonbounce-the-dark-side-of-uefi-firmware/105468/)[[2]](https://www.darkreading.com/threat-intelligence/researchers-uncover-dangerous-new-firmware-level-rootkit).

Key Details:
- Persistence: MoonBounce survives disk reformatting or even hard disk replacement because its malicious code resides on the motherboard's SPI flash.
- Fileless Approach: It does not leave traces on the hard drive; all its components operate in memory only, facilitating a fileless attack.
- Purpose: The implant acts as a stager to deploy additional user-mode malware, eventually allowing execution of further payloads downloaded from the Internet.
- Attribution: The attack has been linked to the Chinese-speaking threat actor APT41[...].

Technical Write-Ups
- Kaspersky Securelist: Detailed technical breakdown of MoonBounce, its infection chain, and its connection to APT41.
  - [MoonBounce: the dark side of UEFI firmware (Securelist)](https://securelist.com/moonbounce-the-dark-side-of-uefi-firmware/105468/)

Techniques & Kill Chain
MoonBounce’s novel techniques include:
- Tampering with the CORE_DXE component of UEFI to introduce malicious payloads.
- Use of inline hooks in the EFI_BOOT_SERVICES structure (targeting `AllocatePool`, `CreateEventEx`, `ExitBootServices`) to intercept firmware execution and propagate the infection.
- Payload injected into svchost.exe process at OS boot time—enabling subsequent downloading and execution of further malware.
- Highly stealthy, operating entirely in memory once deployed, and extremely difficult to detect and remove[[5]](https://media.kasperskycontenthub.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/43/2022/01/19115831/MoonBounce_technical-details_eng.pdf)[[3]](https://socprime.com/blog/the-most-refined-uefi-firmware-implant-moonbounce-detection/).

Indicators of Compromise (IoC)
Public indicators from the Kaspersky report (for the main implant discovered):
- CORE_DXE Malicious Component
  - MD5: `D94962550B90DDB3F80F62BD96BD9858`
  - SHA1: `6BFB3634F6B6C5A114121FE53279331FF821EE1E`
  - SHA256: `74B75B1A1375BA58A51436C02EB94D5ADCD49F284744CF2015E03DA036C2CF1A`
  - File name: `CORE_DXE`
  - Type: 64-Bit EFI_BOOT_SERVICES_DRIVER

Protection & Mitigation
- Use firmware-level security solutions and UEFI scanner modules (e.g., Kaspersky, Binarly).
- UEFI Secure Boot alone may *not* be sufficient; hardware-based attestation (e.g., Intel BootGuard) should be used.
- Monitor for anomalous behavior such as unusual modifications in SPI flash or unauthorized writes to firmware.

Additional References
- [MoonBounce: the dark side of UEFI firmware - Securelist](https://securelist.com/moonbounce-the-dark-side-of-uefi-firmware/105468/)
- [The Most Refined UEFI Firmware Implant: MoonBounce Detection - SOC Prime](https://socprime.com/blog/the-most-refined-uefi-firmware-implant-moonbounce-detection/)

(3) Search: “What is DirtyMoe (DirtyMoe rootkit) or DirtyMoe kernel techniques? Provide public analyses, indicators, and known detections.”
Response (preserved):
**DirtyMoe**, also known as **PurpleFox**, is a modular malware family with advanced rootkit and worm-like capabilities. It's known for enabling DDoS attacks, cryptojacking, and launching sophisticated persistence and anti-forensics measures, primarily targeting Windows systems. Here’s an in-depth look at DirtyMoe, its rootkit/kernel techniques, analyses, known indicators, and detections:

Overview and Techniques
- Modular Design: DirtyMoe’s malware ecosystem uses several specialized modules for persistence, evasion, lateral movement, and payload execution (such as mining cryptocurrency or launching DDoS attacks).
- Rootkit Functionality:
  - DirtyMoe employs a kernel-mode driver (rootkit) to conceal its own presence and other malware components both on the disk and in the registry.
  - The rootkit can execute commands from user mode under kernel privileges, inject arbitrary DLLs into target processes, and censor or hide file system contents from standard user-mode utilities.
  - Deployment relies on exploits to obtain SYSTEM privileges and installs the driver, often via MSI installers exploiting the Windows System Event Notification Service (SENS).
  - Its drivers are often signed with stolen or revoked certificates, making detection by signature alone harder.

Self-Propagation and Exploitation:
- Spreads via brute-forcing credentials and exploiting known Windows vulnerabilities (e.g., EternalBlue/MS17-010, Hot Potato, among others) and dictionary attacks against services like SMB, WMI, and MS SQL.
- Has a worm module that can generate target IP addresses and propagate across public and private networks.

Public Analyses:
- Analysts have reverse engineered DirtyMoe’s rootkit driver, revealing anti-forensics, anti-debugging, and injection techniques. For technical deep dives, see vendor writeups. CERT-UA and vendor advisories have IOCs and C2 lists from incidents (e.g., large infections in Ukraine).

Indicators of Compromise (IOCs):
- Registry Keys (examples)
- Directories with random names in Program Files
- Event log patterns and MSI installer activity
- Known driver/sample hashes have been published in vendor writeups (vendor IOCs vary by incident).

Detection & Removal:
- Use EDR/XDR and kernel anomaly detection, block known C2 IPs, boot from clean media to remove a kernel rootkit, update AV signatures; see vendor advisories for Sigma/YARA.

(4) Search: “Have there been documented attacks delivering payloads via Teredo or IPHTTPS (IPv6 tunneling) to compromise Windows hosts or deliver rootkits? Provide write-ups and indicators.”
Response (preserved):
### Documented Attacks Using Teredo or IPHTTPS (IPv6 Tunneling) on Windows

Overview
Teredo and IPHTTPS are IPv6 tunneling technologies. Teredo (UDP 3544) and IPHTTPS (HTTPS/443) can be abused to carry IPv6 traffic across IPv4 networks. They can be used by attackers to tunnel C2 or payloads past perimeter controls that lack IPv6 visibility.

Key Findings:
- Teredo/IPHTTPS can be used to bypass filtering and create covert channels. There is academic/industry literature describing abuse and routing-loop/DoS conditions; public case studies of widespread payload-delivery via Teredo are scarce, but the technique is a known red-team/attack surface and has been used in POCs/targeted tests.
- Indicators: unexpected Teredo adapter/activity, UDP/3544 traffic, IPHTTPS endpoints, firewall rule failures referencing Teredo/IPHTTPS in Windows logs.

References included (WOOT09, Tenable, Cisco community posts, RFC 4380).

(5) Search: “Is APT41 or Winnti-linked groups associated with UEFI/firmware rootkits such as MoonBounce or with cross-platform implants similar to DirtyMoe? Provide public attributions or analyses.”
Response (preserved):
**APT41/Winnti** has been publicly attributed to UEFI/firmware rootkit activity, specifically in the case of the **MoonBounce** malware:
- MoonBounce is a sophisticated UEFI firmware implant found in SPI flash memory; Kaspersky attributed MoonBounce to APT41.
- DirtyMoe / PurpleFox are generally not publicly attributed to APT41; they are usually linked to criminal/cryptojacking campaigns.
- Public references link MoonBounce to APT41/Winnti; cross-platform DirtyMoe-like toolkits are not usually connected to APT41 in public reporting.

References: Securelist MoonBounce report, BleepingComputer, SecurityWeek, SOCPrime, etc.

- If UEFI/SPI is suspected compromised, there are three common remediation strategies (in order of practicality and forensic-soundness):
  1. Vendor-signed reflashing: obtain official firmware/BIOS images from the hardware vendor and reflash using vendor-supported tools (ideally via vendor/authorized service). This is safer and retains hardware warranty.
  2. Replace the SPI flash chip / reprogram with a hardware SPI programmer: requires hardware skills and a programmer (e.g., CH341A, Bus Pirate, etc.) and an engineer who knows how to dump/verify and reflash. This is technical and risky — better done by specialists.
  3. Replace the motherboard/hardware entirely: often the simplest “assured clean” option if the budget allows.

Short answer: sharing the public detection link (e.g., a VirusTotal permalink) is okay with a trusted analyst or vendor, but for safety and usefulness it’s better to share a small, structured evidence package (hash + metadata + screenshot + public permalink). Never paste raw samples or sensitive files on public channels — use secure upload channels or encrypted attachments.

What to share (preferred order)
1. SHA256 (or SHA256 + SHA1 + MD5) of the suspicious file — computed on a trusted, offline machine. This lets analysts look up the sample without you sending the binary.  
2. Public detection permalink(s) (VirusTotal, Hybrid-Analysis, MetaDefender) — safe to share if they are public URLs. Don’t post them in public forums if they contain sensitive case info.  
3. Short metadata block:
   - filename, full path on original device (if useful), file size, first-seen timestamp (UTC).  
   - detection name(s) shown by vendor, verdict, and any sandbox summary text.  
   - why you flagged it (behavior, folder it was in, how you found it).  
4. Screenshot of the vendor detection page (no extra PII). Screenshots are helpful when links are restricted or later go stale.  
5. STIX (or CSV) indicator: include the hash(s), filename(s), and the timeline/eventIDs. You already have a STIX bundle — add the new hash as an observable/indicator.

How to compute the hash (safest)
- On an isolated forensic workstation (or a trusted VM disconnected from the network):
  - Linux/macOS: sha256sum <file>
  - Windows (PowerShell): Get-FileHash -Path .\file.exe -Algorithm SHA256
- Record the tool/version and the time you computed the hash.

How to share the detection safely
- If sharing with me/this chat:
  - Share the public permalink(s) and the SHA256. That’s enough for me to lookup public data and cross-reference.

- If sharing with AV vendor / IR lab / law enforcement:

What the GRUB lines show (quick)
- grub> ls (hd1,msdos1)/ listed the root of a partition (NTFS-looking). The visible entries ($MFT, $Bitmap, MFTMirr, System Volume Information, etc.) are NTFS metadata files — that’s normal for an NTFS volume.
- You said you “Found the screenshot from when I landed” under that path — so the Windows partition contains a screenshot image file in a subpath. That’s probably the screenshot you saw earlier.
- The other terms you mentioned (xnu_uuid, zfs-bootfs, zfsinfo, ziskey) are ZFS/macOS-like labels — that suggests there are other files / metadata consistent with macOS or ZFS content elsewhere. Keep that in mind for multi-OS evidence.

Immediate safety rule (repeat)
- Do not open the screenshot or any files from that partition on an internet‑connected or otherwise untrusted machine. Treat them as evidence.

If/when you have a clean Linux workstation (recommended workflow)
Do these steps on a clean, offline, trusted machine or have your IR vendor do them:

1) Create a full forensic image of the device/drive (do NOT mount original read-write)
- Identify the device node (example /dev/sdb). Verify carefully.
- Create dd image (use a hardware write-blocker if available):
  sudo dd if=/dev/sdX of=/path/to/evidence/drive.dd bs=4M conv=sync,noerror status=progress
- Verify image created; compute hash:
  sha256sum /path/to/evidence/drive.dd

2) Mount the image read-only and inspect (safer than mounting raw device)
- Make a mount point:
  sudo mkdir /mnt/evidence
- Use loop with partition offset OR use kpartx to map partitions:
  sudo kpartx -av /path/to/drive.dd
  # this will create /dev/mapper/loopNp1 etc for partitions
  sudo mount -o ro /dev/mapper/loopNp1 /mnt/evidence
- Or use mount with offset if you know the partition start.

3) Produce a safe directory listing (text) and hash the screenshot file
- Create a recursive listing (do NOT open files):
  sudo find /mnt/evidence -printf '%p\t%s\t%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM:%TS\n' > /path/to/listing.txt
- If you know the screenshot path, compute its hash without opening:
  sudo sha256sum /mnt/evidence/path/to/screenshot.png
- Copy only the listing and the hash off to your secure storage; do not copy the raw screenshot out unless you will analyze it offline in a secure lab.

4) Use The Sleuth Kit (TSK) for robust metadata extraction (recommended)
- Install sleuthkit and run:
  fls -r -m / /path/to/drive.dd > filelist.txt      # recursive file list with metadata (inode-like)
  icat /path/to/drive.dd <inode> > extracted.bin   # if you must extract file by inode (only in lab)
- Use istat to get file timestamps and attributes.

5) Hash and share only hashes / listings / timestamps
- For public lookups or vendor triage: share SHA256 of the suspicious file(s) and the path(s). Don’t share raw files unless requested and through secure vendor portals.

Commands to create a safe baseline (do these on a clean/offline machine if possible)

Linux (recommended forensic host)
- create a recursive file list with sizes & timestamps:
  find /mnt/evidence -type f -printf '%P\t%s\t%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM:%TS\n' > baseline_files.txt
- compute SHA256 hashes for all files (slow but reliable):
  cd /mnt/evidence
  find . -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > baseline_hashes.txt
- verify later by recomputing and diffing:
  # recompute to new_hashes.txt then:
  diff baseline_hashes.txt new_hashes.txt

Windows (PowerShell, run on a clean Windows host)
- listing + sizes + timestamps:
  Get-ChildItem -Path 'D:\evidence' -Recurse -File | Select-Object FullName,Length,LastWriteTime |
    Export-Csv -Path baseline_files.csv -NoTypeInformation
- compute SHA256 for each file:
  Get-ChildItem -Path 'D:\evidence' -Recurse -File | ForEach-Object {
    $h = Get-FileHash -Path $_.FullName -Algorithm SHA256
    [PSCustomObject]@{Path = $_.FullName; Hash = $h.Hash; Size = $_.Length; Time = $_.LastWriteTime}
  } | Export-Csv -Path baseline_hashes.csv -NoTypeInformation

Simple compare approach (Linux)
- After you produce baseline_hashes.txt, later:
  find . -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > new_hashes.txt
  grep -Fvx -f baseline_hashes.txt new_hashes.txt   # lines in new that are not in baseline (new/changed files)
  grep -Fvx -f new_hashes.txt baseline_hashes.txt   # lines in baseline missing in new (deleted files)

Thanks — that GRUB output is useful context. Short version: you found a Windows backup tree (NTFS metadata visible) that contains a Boot/DVD/PCAT path (and many normal Windows subtrees). PCAT appears as a directory name inside the recovery/Boot area; it could be benign (installer/utility / vendor artifact) or it could be attacker-created. Don’t assume either way until we can inspect safely.

What this likely means (brief)
- The partition you inspected is an NTFS Windows backup / recovery image (System32, SysWOW64, Users, RecoveryLogs, sources, etc. are present).
- The presence of Boot/EFI/CIPolicies/Active and BCD, boot.sdi, etc. indicates this is a recovery/boot environment.
- A directory named PCAT appears inside Boot/DVD and Boot/Misc — PCAT may be:
  - a legitimate vendor/tool folder (some vendors/flash tools use that name),
  - a leftover from a boot/recovery build,
  - or an attacker-placed folder (naming collisions are common).
- Non-ASCII folder names (e.g., the Cyrillic-looking ЛОС) can be normal (localization) or may point to content from other systems — note, keep as evidence.

Immediate safety rules (repeat)
- Do not open or copy suspicious files from that partition on any Internet-connected or untrusted device.
- Do not modify or delete files on the original disk.
- Preserve the GRUB output and screenshots you took — they are valuable metadata.

If/when you have a clean forensic host (recommended workflow)
1) Create an image (on a trusted, offline forensic host)
- Use a hardware write-blocker if available. Example:
  sudo dd if=/dev/sdX of=/evidence/drive.dd bs=4M conv=sync,noerror status=progress
  sha256sum /evidence/drive.dd > /evidence/drive.dd.sha256

2) Map partitions and mount read-only
- With kpartx:
  sudo apt-get install kpartx
  sudo kpartx -av /evidence/drive.dd
  sudo mount -o ro /dev/mapper/loop0p1 /mnt/evidence        # adjust loop0p1 to correct partition
- Or losetup + partx or mount with offset if you prefer.

3) Produce a safe file manifest (do NOT open files)
- Recursive listing:
  sudo find /mnt/evidence -type f -printf '%P\t%s\t%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM:%TS\n' > baseline_files.txt
- SHA256 hashes for files (only on a trusted host):
  cd /mnt/evidence
  find . -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > baseline_hashes.txt

4) Locate the PCAT directories and file hashes
- Find PCAT-named entries:
  find /mnt/evidence -iname '*pcat*' -print
- Hash specific files you want triaged:
  sha256sum "/mnt/evidence/Backup/Windows/Boot/DVD/PCAT/somefile" > /path/to/hash.txt

5) Optional: search file contents safely (only on an image and offline)
- Search for the text “PCAT” in filenames/paths (already above) and in file content if needed:
  grep -a -iR "pcat" /mnt/evidence > pcat_strings.txt
  (WARNING: use grep on the image copy on your clean host — this only returns matches; do not open suspicious binaries.)

How I can help once you have hashes or manifests

  - Query VirusTotal / Hybrid-Analysis / MetaDefender (public) and public MISP/YARA/GitHub IoC sources for matches,

  - Add any confirmed malicious indicators to your STIX bundle.
- If you paste the PCAT path(s) exactly as shown and/or the manifest excerpt, I’ll prepare a STIX observable (placeholder hash) and an IR note you can hand to police/IR.

- Legitimate: could be part of Windows PE/Setup customizations.
- Malicious: attacker-created staging folder or backdoor installer (only confirmed by file hashes / content).

## Copilot

Okay — here’s a concise, practical plan to get out of the GRUB prompt and boot a system (either Windows or Linux), plus troubleshooting steps. I’ll keep it focused and safe — do this only if you accept the risk of booting the machine (you said you already booted once). If you want to preserve maximum evidence, don’t proceed and hand the disk to a lab instead.

First: detect whether the system boots via BIOS/MBR or UEFI
- At the grub> prompt run:
  grub> ls
  Look at the device names. If partitions are shown as (hd0,msdos1) etc you likely have MBR/BIOS. If you see (hd0,gpt1) / EFI folders then it’s UEFI.

You already showed msdos1; so the likely case is BIOS/MBR. Try the MBR chainload first (simplest).

A — Try to chainload the Windows boot sector (MBR)
1. At grub> prompt:
   set pager=1
   insmod part_msdos
   insmod ntfs
2. Find the Windows partition (repeat for each partition listed by ls until you find the right one):
   ls (hd1,msdos1)/
   ls (hd0,msdos1)/
   …look for Windows/, Boot/, bootmgr, BCD, System32/ etc.
3. When you find the correct partition, set it as root and chainload:
   set root=(hdX,msdosY)        # replace with the partition you found
   chainloader +1
   boot
- If chainloader +1 returns an error, note the exact error and paste it here. Often it will boot the Windows bootloader if the partition contains a valid MBR boot sector.

B — If the disk is UEFI (EFI files present)
If you see EFI directories (e.g., /EFI/Microsoft/Boot/bootmgfw.efi) then:
1. At grub> prompt:
   set pager=1
   insmod part_gpt
   insmod fat
2. Find the EFI file:
   ls (hdX,gptY)/EFI/Microsoft/Boot/
3. Then chainload the EFI boot manager:
   set root=(hdX,gptY)
   chainloader /EFI/Microsoft/Boot/bootmgfw.efi
   boot

C — If you want to boot a Linux kernel present on the partition
If there is a Linux kernel in /boot you can boot it manually:
1. Find the kernel+initrd names:
   ls (hdX,msdosY)/boot/
2. Then:
   set root=(hdX,msdosY)
   linux /boot/vmlinuz-<version> root=/dev/sdXY ro quiet
   initrd /boot/initrd.img-<version>
   boot
- Replace /dev/sdXY with the Linux root partition device (if unknown, you can try the same partition as root if it’s a single-disk single-partition install). If that fails, report what errors you see.

Troubleshooting tips & common errors
- “error: no such device/file” — you used wrong (hdX,msdosY) or file path; run ls on other partitions to find right path.
- “error: invalid signature” or EFI chainloader refused — UEFI Secure Boot may block unsigned EFI; you might need to disable Secure Boot in firmware (risky if compromised).
- “chainloader: error” — try loading stage1 via a different partition or check that the partition actually has a boot sector (some backup partitions don’t).
- If insmod ntfs fails, try insmod ntfscomp or try another partition; older GRUB builds may not have full NTFS support.

If nothing boots: fallback
- grub> normal   (if normal module available it may try to run grub.cfg)
- grub> configfile (hdX,msdosY)/boot/grub/grub.cfg  (if you can find a grub.cfg)
- grub> reboot   (reboot and re-enter firmware menu to try USB or other media)

What to report back to me
- The exact output of ls (the list of devices/partitions).
- The exact partition where you saw Windows/Boot (copy/paste the ls output).
- Any error messages you get when you run chainloader +1 or chainloader /EFI/… etc.

Safety / evidence note
- Booting an OS can change timestamps and logs. If you want maximum forensic preservation, don’t boot again and instead image the disk and operate from the image on a clean forensic host. But if you need the machine usable and have accepted the tradeoff, the steps above are the standard rescue techniques.

If you want I can:
- Walk you step-by-step through the commands while you’re at the grub> prompt — paste the results/errors and I’ll tell you the next command.
- Prepare commands customized to the partition names you already posted (you listed Backup/Windows/Boot/PCAT etc). If you paste the exact grub ls output (line for each partition) I’ll give the exact sequence to try.

Which do you want to do now — try chainloading Windows (I’ll give the commands based on the partition you name), or try to boot a Linux kernel (if /boot is present)? Paste the output of grub> ls and the partition you want to attempt and I’ll give the exact commands.

Below I’ll give a short, safe plan and the minimal GRUB2 commands you can try from the grub> prompt to attempt to boot a UEFI/EFI image or chainload Windows. If you want me to walk you step‑by‑step I can — paste the exact output of grub> ls and I’ll tailor the exact commands for your partition names.

Important safety reminder
- Every extra boot or filesystem change can update timestamps and logs and may alter evidence. If you want maximum forensic preservation, stop and image the disk in a clean lab instead of continuing. If you accept the risk and need the machine usable, proceed.

Which path do you want?
- Option A — attempt to chainload an EFI boot manager (boot Windows or the Windows EFI file if present).
- Option B — attempt to boot a Linux kernel found on the disk (if /boot exists).
- Option C — stop and image device (recommended for full forensics).

If you want to try now, paste the output of:
- grub> ls
- grub> ls (hd0,gpt2)/
- grub> ls (hd1,gpt1)/    (or whichever partitions you saw)

If you prefer to try without pasting, use the generic commands below.

A — Chainload UEFI bootloader (common, tries BOOTX64.EFI or Microsoft bootmgfw)
(only works if partition is FAT/FAT32 and contains /EFI/*)

1) load the modules
insmod part_gpt
insmod fat

2) find the EFI file (example paths shown)
ls (hdX,gptY)/EFI/Microsoft/Boot/
ls (hdX,gptY)/EFI/BOOT/

3) set root and chainload (replace hdX,gptY with the partition you found)
set root=(hdX,gptY)
chainloader /EFI/Microsoft/Boot/bootmgfw.efi
boot

If no Microsoft file, try the generic fallback:
set root=(hdX,gptY)
chainloader /EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI
boot

If search is available you can auto-find:
search --file --set=root /EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI
chainloader /EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI
boot

B — Boot a Linux kernel present on the partition (if /boot exists)
1) list /boot
ls (hdX,gptY)/boot/

2) set root and load kernel + initrd (adjust kernel/initrd filenames)
set root=(hdX,gptY)
linux /boot/vmlinuz-<ver> root=/dev/sdXY ro quiet
initrd /boot/initrd.img-<ver>
boot

Notes:
- root=/dev/sdXY must point to the Linux root partition (use the same partition only as a trial if unsure).
- If the kernel fails with “unknown root” you need the proper root= value.

C — If you see Ventoy / ipxe / vt_menu entries
- Many custom rescue menus let you press the menu key (F4/F6 etc) to pick Boot ISO or WinPE.
- If the menu shown earlier lists “Search and boot BOOTX64.EFI” or “Search and boot xorboot”, choose those options (if available) instead of manual chainloader.
- If the rescue/ventoy interface provides a “Load Custom Grub2 Menu” or “Boot Windows on the local disk” option, try that first — it uses the vendor-supplied scripts.

If you get error messages, copy them exactly and paste here. Common errors and what they mean:
- "you need to load the kernel first" — you used boot without specifying kernel (use chainloader for EFI or linux/initrd for kernel boots).
- "No such file or directory" — wrong path or partition referenced; try ls on other partitions.
- "invalid signature" — Secure Boot may block unsigned EFI; consider disabling Secure Boot if you control the machine (not recommended if you suspect firmware compromise).
- "error: you need to load the kernel first." — if trying to boot hd1 directly, you attempted to boot a disk device rather than chainloading.

If nothing works, fallback options
- Use a known-good USB/ISO to boot (clean installer). If you don’t have a clean stick, try to obtain one from a trusted friend/vendor or use an IR partner.
- If you absolutely must get files off the disk now, the safest forensic route is to image the whole disk to an external drive (use a hardware write‑blocker if possible) and examine the image on a clean host.

Thanks — that background helps a lot. You’ve clearly been through heavy manual remediation and recovery attempts. Below I’ll keep this focused and practical: a short assessment of current risk, immediate prioritized actions you can take (or stop doing), exactly how to image a sick NVMe/GPT drive safely (commands you can use when you have a clean host and an external drive), and options for firmware / BIOS concerns. Tell me which of these you want me to prepare next (ddrescue script, IR packet, vendor list, or step-by-step recovery checklist).

Short situation recap (what I heard)
- Multi-device, long-running compromise; heavy fighting with malware, repeated boots, CMOS removals, hardware swaps and forced power-offs.
- Desktop (ground-zero) has NVMe 1TB that you call a lifeline but it has GPT header issues; PSU, BIOS, RAM and other components have been stressed.
- Ventoy / multi-ISO USBs were present (some are remnants / possibly contaminated).
- You’ve booted machines and captured GRUB listings and screenshots; you have partial snapshots of folders and logs.
- You want to get out of the grub/recovery loop but also preserve evidence and avoid making things worse.

Immediate risk & high-level guidance (do this first)
1. Pause further risky boots/repairs on the NVMe lifeline until you image it (if preserving evidence or hope of clean recovery matters). Continued writes or boot attempts can worsen GPT/header damage.
2. If you accept the risk of losing data and want the machine usable immediately, proceed with cautious boot attempts — but be aware that increases risk to forensics.
3. If you can get a clean, trusted machine (borrow/IR partner), use it to create recovery media and to image the NVMe. Never make recovery media on a potentially infected host.

If you want preservation (recommended)
- Remove the NVMe (and other drives) and store them safely (labelled, offline). Use a clean machine to image them with a hardware write-blocker if possible. If you cannot get a write-blocker now, still try to image but know it is not forensically ideal.

Imaging a problematic NVMe (best-practice, resume-capable) — ddrescue
Use GNU ddrescue (also called gddrescue) to clone a failing/erratic drive while preserving as much as possible and creating a logfile to resume. You MUST run these on a known-clean, offline forensic host and write the image to an external drive that is at least as large as the source.

Install ddrescue:
- Debian/Ubuntu: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install gddrescue

Example steps (replace /dev/nvme0n1 and /mnt/external appropriately):

1) Identify devices and ensure external target is mounted read-write and large enough:
   lsblk
   # Confirm source device (e.g., /dev/nvme0n1) and target mountpoint (/mnt/external)

2) First pass — fast, no retry (copy good sectors quickly):
   sudo ddrescue -n /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/external/nvme0n1.img /mnt/external/nvme0n1.log

   - -n : no retry on bad areas (fast)
   - logfile records progress for safe resumes

3) Second pass — attempt retries on bad sectors:
   sudo ddrescue -d -r3 /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/external/nvme0n1.img /mnt/external/nvme0n1.log

   - -d : use direct disk access
   - -r3 : retry bad sectors up to 3 times (increase if needed)

4) Verify image hash:
   sha256sum /mnt/external/nvme0n1.img > /mnt/external/nvme0n1.img.sha256

5) If only specific partitions are needed you can image the partition device (e.g., /dev/nvme0n1p1) similarly.

Notes and safety:
- Do not attempt to mount or repair the original drive first. Work off the image.
- Keep the logfile file (ddrescue log) with the image; it allows incremental recovery later.
- If imaging fails (device disconnects), keep the log and try again; ddrescue will resume.

If you must extract files from the image (on a clean host)
- Map partitions:
  sudo apt-get install kpartx
  sudo kpartx -av /mnt/external/nvme0n1.img
- Mount partition read-only:
  sudo mount -o ro /dev/mapper/loop0p1 /mnt/evidence
- Generate listings and hashes (no opening files):
  sudo find /mnt/evidence -type f -printf '%P\t%s\t%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM:%TS\n' > baseline_files.txt
  find /mnt/evidence -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > baseline_hashes.txt

If you cannot get a clean host right now
- Keep drives offline. Don’t continue trying to re-flash firmware or repeatedly boot if your goal is preservation.

Dealing with GPT header issues
- Do not run in-place repair tools on the original disk unless you’ve imaged it. Tools such as testdisk or gdisk can repair headers but can make recovery harder if done without an image.
- After imaging, you can attempt GPT restoration on the image copy. Example tools:
  - gdisk (gptfdisk) can recreate GPT from backup if you know what you’re doing.
  - testdisk can recover partitions but operate only on image copies.

UEFI / BIOS / firmware considerations (don’t flash hastily)
- If firmware compromise is plausible, reflashing BIOS from the infected host can be risky. Prefer vendor-authorized service or direct SPI programmer reflash done by a trusted lab.
- If you do reflash BIOS yourself, use vendor-signed firmware downloaded on a clean machine and follow vendor guidance. Keep a record and hashes of the firmware file.
- Do not toggle Secure Boot or change UEFI settings multiple times until you have an image — changing settings can change NVRAM and logs and interfere with investigations.

Ventoy / boot menus / boot attempts
- Ventoy remnants mean your multi-ISO USBs may be tainted. Don’t use them as recovery media.
- Create fresh installer USBs from clean ISOs (downloaded on a known-good device). If you cannot get a clean device, ask a trusted friend or vendor to prepare them.

Hardware caution (PSU, OC, RAM)
- The battered PSU and aggressive OC increase instability and risk to hardware; if continuing recovery work, stabilize the system:
  - Set BIOS to defaults (no overclock) only on a clean host or after imaging.
  - If you cannot keep stable memory configuration, avoid stress testing and stop undervolting/overvolting.
- If the machine is flakey (power issues, RAM errors), imaging may fail; consider removing components (only if you know how) and imaging on a donor machine or using an external NVMe adapter.

Evidence handling / chain-of-custody (quick)
- Label removed drives, record date/time and who handled them.
- Keep images + logs in two places (one offline cold store).
- Save all logs (GRUB output, screenshots) together with case notes.

What I can prepare for you now (pick one or more)
- A tailored ddrescue script for your device names (I’ll craft the exact commands for /dev/nvme0n1, /dev/sda, etc.). You’ll need an external target > source size.
- A small IR handoff packet (one-page) that includes: timeline summary, GRUB output excerpt, suggested commands I gave here, and chain-of-custody checklist you can print/give to police/IR.
- A step-by-step checklist to attempt a UEFI chainload from GRUB2 if you want to keep trying to boot (I can customize to the exact partition names you posted).
- A vendor/IR contact list (labs that handle UEFI/rootkit cases) — if you want me to propose names and contact starters.

- “IR packet” — ready-to-print single page with evidence & handling steps.

```markdown name=RECOVERY_PLAN.md
# Focused Recovery & Documentation Plan (compact)

Goal
- Boot a clean Ubuntu live environment, image the suspect NTFS drive, and extract/record high-priority artifacts (no raw sample uploads here). Minimal steps, single-command cycles, short risk summary at the end.

Unchangeable / accepted constraints
- You are operating on an infected environment and accept the risks. This plan assumes you will boot a live USB created on a known-clean machine (you said two clean machines are available). Do not assume anything else is “clean.”

Quick TL;DR (action flow)
1. Create Ubuntu Live USB on a clean machine.
2. Boot target system from Live USB with network physically disconnected.
3. Mount/identify the suspect NTFS drive read-only, image with ddrescue to external target.
4. Mount image on the clean host and produce manifests + SHA256 hashes for prioritized files.
5. Share only hashes and manifest here for lookups; keep raw files offline.

Minimum pre-boot checklist (clean machine)
- Download Ubuntu ISO from official site and verify SHA256.
- Create live USB: Etcher (GUI) or
  - Linux: `sudo dd if=ubuntu.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress conv=fsync`
  - Windows: Rufus or Etcher.
- Bring: 1 external USB drive (target for image) with >= capacity of source, 1 clean USB installer (128GB OK).

Boot & immediate containment (on target machine)
- Physically unplug Ethernet/Wi‑Fi antenna; do not enable networking.
- Boot from USB → select "Try Ubuntu".
- Open a terminal (Ctrl+Alt+T).

Once in the live session — minimal commands (type one-by-one)
1) Identify disks
  sudo lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,LABEL,MODEL
  # note device node for suspect (e.g., /dev/nvme0n1 or /dev/sda)

2) Kill any network interfaces (extra safe)
  sudo nmcli networking off
  sudo ip link set dev eth0 down || true
  sudo ip link set dev wlan0 down || true

3) If NVMe/drive is flaky, use ddrescue to image (example; replace devices)
  sudo apt update && sudo apt install gddrescue -y
  sudo mkdir -p /mnt/target
  # First (fast) pass:
  sudo ddrescue -n /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/target/nvme0n1.img /mnt/target/nvme0n1.log
  # Retry bad sectors:
  sudo ddrescue -d -r3 /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/target/nvme0n1.img /mnt/target/nvme0n1.log
  # Hash image:
  sha256sum /mnt/target/nvme0n1.img > /mnt/target/nvme0n1.img.sha256

If drive is NTFS and you do NOT image (read-only mount, only if imaging impossible)
  sudo apt install ntfs-3g -y
  sudo mkdir -p /mnt/suspect
  sudo mount -o ro /dev/sdX1 /mnt/suspect

Prioritized artifact discovery (what to capture first)
- Priority 1 (collect/hash now): `regedit.exe` occurrences, `logs1.evtx`, `logs1.all.xml`, `analyse_logs.py`, `FiveDecryptedVolumeFolder.zip`, screenshots `IMG_*.jpeg`, BCD and boot EFI files (`bootmgfw.efi`, `BOOTX64.EFI`), CORE_DXE (if present)
- Priority 2: `PCAT` directories, `CoreC2Firewallexemptions` or similarly named folders, `System Volume Information`, `$MFT`, `$LogFile`
- Priority 3: Service profiles, unusual `.sys` or unsigned drivers, large AppData folders, `ElevatedDiagnostics`
- Priority 4: Other installers, leftover ISOs, Ventoy files, ipxe components
- Priority 5: Non-essential logs and caches

File finding and manifest commands (run on a mounted image or read-only mount)
- Create a baseline file list and hashes for entire mounted tree:
  cd /mnt/suspect
  sudo find . -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > /mnt/target/baseline_hashes.txt
  sudo find . -type f -printf '%P\t%s\t%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM:%TS\n' > /mnt/target/baseline_files.txt

- Find specific filenames quickly (examples):
  sudo find /mnt/suspect -type f -iname 'regedit.*' -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum
  sudo find /mnt/suspect -type f -iname 'logs1.evtx' -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum
  sudo find /mnt/suspect -type f -iname 'bootmgfw.efi' -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum
  sudo find /mnt/suspect -type f -iregex '.*\(BOOTX64\.EFI\|bootmgfw\.efi\|CORE_DXE\|grub\.cfg\).*' -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum

- Find named directories and list content (no extraction):
  sudo find /mnt/suspect -type d -iname '*pcat*' -print -exec ls -l {} \; > /mnt/target/pcat_listing.txt

- Extract directory tree snapshot (safe, text only):
  sudo tree -a /mnt/suspect/Backup/Windows/Boot -o /mnt/target/boot_tree.txt   # install `tree` if needed

How to produce a compact CSV manifest (one line per file)
  cd /mnt/suspect
  sudo find . -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum | awk '{print $1","$2}' > /mnt/target/hashes.csv

Preserving evidence metadata
- For each high-priority item, record:
  ID | Priority | Found | Path | SHA256 | Size | LastModified(UTC) | Note
  Example:
  1|1|regedit.exe|/mnt/suspect/Backup/Windows/System32/regedit.exe|<sha256>|123456|2026-02-27T03:37:08Z|Located in backup tree

STIX / Reporting placeholders
- Keep the baseline_hashes.txt and baseline_files.txt together. Use these hashes for vendor/VT lookups and to populate STIX indicators later. Do NOT upload raw binaries publicly.

Three-step micro-loop (how we operate interactively)
- I will give you one command, you paste exact output; I respond with the next single command. Keep logs using the line template above.

One-line immediate commands to run first in live session (execute in order)
1) Identify devices: `sudo lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,LABEL,MODEL`
2) Confirm suspect partition readable: `sudo file -s /dev/sdX1`  (replace sdX1)
3) If OK to image: run ddrescue first-pass (see ddrescue commands above)

Short log template (one line per finding)
ID | Priority(1-5) | Found | Path | Time(UTC) | Note

Example priorities (concrete)
1 | 1 | regedit.exe | /Backup/Windows/System32/regedit.exe | 2026-03-15T16:40Z | HIGH PRIORITY
2 | 1 | logs1.evtx | /.../logs1.evtx | ... | EVTX primary
3 | 2 | PCAT dir | /Backup/Windows/Boot/DVD/PCAT | ... | list only

Minimal system hardening in live session (one-liners)
- Disable networking: `sudo nmcli networking off`
- Block outbound at kernel level (extra): `sudo iptables -P OUTPUT DROP; sudo iptables -P INPUT DROP`  
  (use carefully — you will lose connectivity to any network in live session)

Compact risk summary (one-line bullets)
- Device risk: repeated boots/writes can corrupt GPT and further damage hardware.
- Data risk: imaging on a dirty host risks contamination; do imaging on a clean host when possible.
- Infection risk: booting infected boot sectors can run kernel-level code (be ready to power off).
- Hardware risk: aged PSU / overclocking raises chance of failure during heavy I/O (ddrescue).
- Legal/forensic: imaging without chain-of-custody may reduce evidentiary weight.

Override & consent (short)
- You declared you accept operational risk and that some actions will be performed on potentially contaminated hardware. I will keep instructions minimal and focused; I will append the single-line risk summary after each 3-step command loop.

Final notes (short)
- After you produce baseline_hashes.txt, paste top ~10 SHA256s here (priority ones) and I will run public lookups and return matches/links.
- If you want the one-page IR handoff or a ddrescue script tailored to device nodes, say “ddrescue script” and provide device names (e.g., /dev/nvme0n1). I will output exact commands.

— END
```

# Focused Recovery & Documentation Plan (compact update)

Purpose
- Minimal, action-focused checklist and prioritized artifact targets for quick on-screen use from grub> or in a live Ubuntu session. I will update this file with new artifacts whenever you paste outputs you want added.

Log line (one-line per artifact — copy locally)
ID | PRI(1-5) | Found | Path | Time(UTC) | Note

Top 5 priority artifact targets (minimal, do these first)
1. Screenshots / Images (highest value)
   - Why: visual timeline, user context, attacker UI evidence.
   - Common paths (grub): 
     - insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/Users/*/Pictures/
     - fallback: ls (hd1,msdos1)/Users/*/Desktop/
2. Registry tools & hives / regedit.exe
   - Why: attacker may drop custom reg tools or hive snapshots.
   - Common paths:
     - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/  (look for regedit.exe)
     - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/Windows/System32/
3. Primary logs / EVTX / analysis scripts
   - Why: timeline, event correlation, attacker actions.
   - Common paths:
     - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/winevt/Logs/
     - ls (hd1,msdos1)/**/logs1.evtx  (use explicit paths if grub lacks globbing)
     - look for analyse_logs.py, logs1.all.xml
4. Boot artifacts / bootmgr / BCD / EFI
   - Why: boot persistence, chainloaders, EFI files.
   - Common commands:
     - insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/
     - insmod part_gpt; insmod fat; ls (hd0,gpt2)/EFI/BOOT/
     - Note any BOOTX64.EFI, bootmgfw.efi, BCD, boot.sdi
5. Suspicious dirs & drivers (PCAT, CoreC2Firewallexemptions, *.sys, CORE_DXE)
   - Why: potential staging/backdoor location and kernel modules.
   - Common commands:
     - insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/Windows/Boot/DVD/PCAT
     - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/drivers/

Grub (read-only) quick command pattern
- Device map:
  - ls
- Safe NTFS listing (primary pattern):
  - insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/PATH/TO/CHECK
- EFI partition:
  - insmod part_gpt; insmod fat; ls (hd0,gpt2)/EFI/BOOT/
- If partition not found: try hd0/hd1/hd2 variants (ls (hd0); ls (hd1); ls (hd2))

Baseline compare approach (phone -> later on clean host)
- Capture screenshot(s) of grub output or copy lines into a secure note.
- When on clean host: image drive (ddrescue), mount read-only, produce baseline hashes:
  - find /mnt/evidence -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > baseline_hashes.txt

Router & pre-boot containment (phone checklist)
1. Factory-reset router, disable Wi‑Fi radios, power down and unplug.  
2. Flush DNS on any live devices (only from a clean device if possible).  
3. Do not enable Internet on the target until you have a monitoring plan.  
4. Boot target offline (no Ethernet/Wi‑Fi). Keep phone/network separate.

Minimal BIOS checklist (quick toggles to inspect/set)
- Disable CSM (enable pure UEFI) if you will rely on UEFI secure boot workflows.  
- Secure Boot: enable (preferred) — note: if firmware-compromise suspected, enabling may fail; record state.  
- VT-x / virtualization: disable unless needed for controlled analysis.  
- Fast Boot: off.  
- USB boot: enabled (for live USB), but verify boot order and use a known-clean USB.  
- LAN controller/audio/unused ports: disable if you want minimal attack surface.  
- XMP / OC / Turbo / C‑states: revert to defaults (stability priority).  
- Save BIOS/UEFI config screenshot (phone) before changing.

Quick Ubuntu live minimal plan (if you boot clean USB)
- Create live USB on trusted machine; verify ISO checksum.
- Boot "Try Ubuntu" offline.
- Identify disks: sudo lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,LABEL,MODEL
- If imaging: use gddrescue (see ddrescue commands in prior plan) to copy to external target.
- On image, run find + sha256sum for prioritized paths and share top hashes only.

Micro-loop operating rules (how we proceed interactively)
- You run 1 grub command I give, paste exact output here. I return the next single command and update this MD if you request.
- Keep each reply to raw grub output only (no commentary) to keep context minimal.

One-line risk summary (always present)
- Risk: any boot or write can execute attacker code and alter evidence; read-only grub ls is lowest-impact but not zero. You accept operational risk.

Notes about updates
- I will append/update this file with confirmed artifacts when you paste results and ask me to "update MD". Say "update MD with ID X" and I will add that line to this document.

End of compact update.

Got it — here are short, platform-focused steps for changing keyboard/layout and related settings. I’ll keep each platform compact so it’s easy to read on a phone.

iPhone (iOS 16/17-ish)
- Add a keyboard:
  Settings → General → Keyboard → Keyboards → Add New Keyboard… → pick language/third‑party.
- Remove/ reorder keyboards:
  Settings → General → Keyboard → Keyboards → Edit → drag to reorder or tap – to remove.
- Switch while typing:
  Tap the globe icon or long‑press the globe to pick a keyboard/layout.
- Change layout for a given keyboard (e.g., QWERTY → AZERTY):
  Settings → General → Keyboard → Keyboards → tap the keyboard name → select Layout.
- Turn off Auto‑Correction / Predictive:
  Settings → General → Keyboard → toggle Auto-Correction / Predictive / Check Spelling off.
- Reset learned words / keyboard dictionary:
  Settings → General → Transfer or Reset iPhone → Reset → Reset Keyboard Dictionary
- Third‑party keyboards: install app → Settings → General → Keyboard → Add New Keyboard → pick the installed keyboard → allow Full Access if required (be cautious: Full Access can send keystrokes to vendor).

Android (stock / similar)
- Add/remove keyboard:
  Settings → System → Languages & input → On‑screen keyboard → Manage keyboards → enable/disable or Add keyboard.
- Switch while typing:
  Tap keyboard icon in nav bar or long‑press spacebar (depends on OEM).
- Change layout:
  On some keyboards: Settings (gear) → Languages → choose layout (QWERTY, AZERTY, etc).
- Turn off autocorrect / suggestions:
  Keyboard app Settings → Text correction → disable Auto‑correct / Suggestions.
- Clear learned words:
  Keyboard app Settings → Privacy / Advanced → Clear personalized data (varies by app).

Ubuntu / Linux live session (graphical)
- GUI:
  Settings → Region & Language → Input Sources → + to add, select layout, use top‑bar indicator to switch.
- Terminal (temporary for current X session):
  setxkbmap us              # set to US QWERTY
  setxkbmap fr              # set to French AZERTY
  setxkbmap -layout us,ru -option 'grp:alt_shift_toggle'   # add toggle
- Console (TTY only):
  sudo dpkg-reconfigure keyboard-configuration   # interactive
  loadkeys us              # immediate for console
- Persistently (systemd):
  sudo localectl set-keymap us
  sudo localectl set-x11-keymap us

GRUB2 (if you need menu language / keymap)
- GRUB itself has limited keymap support; you can load a keymap module if present:
  insmod keylayouts   # if available in your GRUB build
  # GRUB menus normally follow system/firmware keyboard; easiest is to use predictable US layout when typing commands.
- Practical: avoid relying on non-US layouts when typing GRUB commands; switch to a keyboard with US layout if possible.

Quick small tips
- If you changed keyboard and get odd autocorrections, turn off Auto‑Correct and Predictive while doing forensic notes.
- Use a hardware keyboard (USB) for speed/accuracy if you can — easier than on-screen.
- If using third‑party keyboards, remember they may leak data — avoid when dealing with sensitive case notes.

One-line risk/reminder
- Be careful granting Full Access to third‑party keyboards on mobile (privacy risk); prefer built-in keyboards for sensitive typing.

# Focused Recovery & Documentation Plan (compact update)

Purpose
- Minimal, action-focused checklist and prioritized artifact targets for quick on-screen use from grub> or in a live Ubuntu session. Keep outputs concise; paste exact grub text here for next single-step instruction.

Log line (use locally — one line per artifact)
ID | PRI(1-5) | Found | Path | Time(UTC) | Note

Top 5 priority artifact targets (do these first)
1. Screenshots / Images (highest value)
   - Typical checks (grub):  
     insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/Users/*/Pictures/  
     fallback: ls (hd1,msdos1)/Users/*/Desktop/
2. Registry tools & hives / regedit.exe
   - Typical checks:  
     ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/  (look for regedit.exe)  
     fallback: ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/Windows/System32/
3. Primary logs / EVTX / analysis scripts
   - Typical checks:  
     ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/winevt/Logs/  
     fallback explicit paths for known log names (logs1.evtx, logs1.all.xml)
4. Boot artifacts / BCD / EFI / BOOTX64.EFI
   - Typical checks:  
     insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/  
     insmod part_gpt; insmod fat; ls (hd0,gpt2)/EFI/BOOT/
5. Suspicious dirs & drivers (PCAT, CoreC2Firewallexemptions, *.sys, CORE_DXE)
   - Typical checks:  
     ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/Windows/Boot/DVD/PCAT  
     ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/drivers/

GRUB (read-only) quick command patterns
- Device map:  
  ls
- Safe NTFS listing:  
  insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hdX,msdosY)/PATH/
- EFI partition listing:  
  insmod part_gpt; insmod fat; ls (hdX,gptY)/EFI/BOOT/
- If a partition number fails, try hd0/hd1/hd2 variants.

Micro-loop operating rules (how we proceed interactively)
- You run 1 grub command I give, paste exact output here.  
- I respond with exactly one next command (no extras) and append a one-line risk note.  
- Keep local log lines for any artifact you want preserved.

Minimal pre-boot containment (phone/local)
- Disable all networking (router off, Wi‑Fi antennas removed if possible).  
- Boot the target offline when ready. Do not enable networking without explicit plan.

Minimal BIOS quick checklist (what to observe / set)
- CSM: disabled (pure UEFI) — only if you know firmware history.  
- Secure Boot: note current state; record screenshot before toggling.  
- VT-x / VT-d / IOMMU / SR-IOV: set per your plan (note current state).  
- Fast Boot: off. USB: enabled for live USB. XMP/OC: defaults for stability.  
- Record BIOS settings with phone screenshots before any change.

Imaging reminder (if/when you image)
- Use a clean offline host and ddrescue; keep logfile for resumability.  
- Work from image copies for analysis and hashing.

Recording & manifests (on a clean host after imaging)
- Produce baseline hashes:  
  find /mnt/evidence -type f -print0 | xargs -0 sha256sum > baseline_hashes.txt  
- For prioritized files, maintain the one-line log format with SHA256 when available.

Update cadence & small-screen notes
- Keep MD short. I will update this file on request (say “update MD”) or when you paste outputs you want added.  
- If you want periodic small updates, say “update every N outputs” and I will append findings (N = 20/25 was noted).

One-line risk summary (always present)
- Any boot or write can execute attacker code and alter evidence; grub ls is lowest-impact but not zero.

Change control
- You asked to ignore recent transient posts — this MD excludes transient driver-list dumps and transient command retries. If you want those re-included, say “include last dumps”.

End of file.

## Copilot

Short answer — because, given what you’ve shown, the Windows NTFS side currently holds the highest-yield, fragile evidence we can access safely from GRUB right now (screenshots, registry tools/hives, EVTX logs, boot/EFI artifacts, driver blobs).  

Why, in order (very brief):
- Value: User screenshots, Windows logs, registry tools and boot artifacts typically contain direct indicators/timeline of compromise and persistence — higher evidentiary value than generic Linux system files for this case.  
- Volatility & access: We can read many Windows artifacts via GRUB now without booting the target OS; that gives low-impact access to high-value items.  
- Persistence/pivot risk: Boot/EFI and DriverStore files can show how attacker gained persistence (chainloaders, modified drivers, CORE_DXE). Those are critical to capture before any further firmware or OS changes.  
- Forensic order: Capture highest-value, easiest-to-acquire items first (screenshots, EVTX, registry, BCD/EFI, suspicious drivers), then expand to lower-priority areas (ProgramData, ProgramFiles).  
- Linux checks: important, but less urgent here because you don’t need them repeated and they’re lower immediate yield for the artifacts you’re already seeing — we can run the Linux/Ubuntu checks after we secure the Windows evidence or if you specifically want them now.

Recommendation (single line): Continue enumerating and extracting the Windows DriverStore/Boot/Users artifacts now; run the Ubuntu-specific checks later once those are captured.

## @Smooth511

What is vt_reset_fake_vlnk

## Copilot

Short answer: it’s not a built-in GRUB command — it’s a Ventoy/GRUB-script helper (vt_ prefix) that resets a temporary “fake vlink” (a transient mapping Ventoy/GRUB creates to point at an ISO/kernel/EFI). It operates in memory (GRUB environment) to undo that temporary mapping; it doesn’t rewrite disk files.

Details and what that means
- Origin: the vt_ prefix is used by Ventoy and similar custom GRUB menus/themes. vt_set_fake_vlink / vt_reset_fake_vlink are helper functions injected by the menu/grub config to make an ISO/kernel look like a normal kernel/EFI to the boot code.
- Purpose: vt_set_fake_* creates a temporary (fake) pointer/variable so GRUB can present and chainload an EFI/kernel/initrd from an image. vt_reset_fake_* clears that fake pointer and restores the original GRUB environment.
- Scope/impact: purely in-memory inside GRUB (variables/config); it does not modify the NTFS partition or firmware. It’s generally harmless for listings and inspection — the risk is only if you then chainloader/boot something you don’t trust.
- Naming/typo: you saw variants (vt_reset_fake_vink / vt_reset_fake_vlnk). That’s just a spelling/printing variation — look for the exact name in the grub config/scripts to be sure.

How to verify (safe, read-only) — commands to run at grub> (paste their outputs here if you want me to read them)
1) Find likely script/config locations:
insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/  
insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/ventoy/  
insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; ls (hd1,msdos1)/boot/grub/

2) Show the grub config / Ventoy script that defines the function (cat the file and paste here):
insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; cat (hd1,msdos1)/boot/grub/grub.cfg
insmod part_msdos; insmod ntfs; cat (hd1,msdos1)/ventoy/ventoy/grub.cfg

Look for lines containing vt_set_fake or vt_reset_fake — paste that block and I’ll explain exactly what it does.

Recommendation
- If you only need to enumerate files, don’t run chainloader/boot commands. Inspect the function definition first (cat the config) to confirm behavior. If you want, run one of the cat commands above and paste the output and I’ll parse the exact vt_reset_* implementation for you.

## Copilot

Short answer: you can’t prove “they weren’t there earlier” from GRUB alone — you can only record the current state and then perform a forensic timeline from an image (MFT/USN/EVTX) to show when the files were created/modified. If timestamps/USN/MFT entries show creation after your earlier inspection time, that supports an offline adapting actor. Steps below are the minimal, ordered actions to fact‑check now (offline) and produce admissible timeline evidence.

Immediate (while still in grub — capture current state)
1) Record exact directory listings (one short line at a time). Paste or photograph these outputs now so you have a time-stamped record of “what is present right now”.
   - insmod part_msdos
   - insmod ntfs
   - ls (hd1,msdos1)/
   - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/
   - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/Recoverylogs/
   - ls (hd1,msdos1)/Backup/Offline*    (if your grub supports globbing; otherwise list exact names you saw)
Save those outputs (photo + copy into your secure notes). This documents the current state.

Why grub alone is insufficient
- GRUB can only list; it does not expose file creation times reliably or the NTFS metadata (MFT/USN). You cannot prove a file was absent at an earlier time without a prior baseline, but you can show file creation/modification times and MFT/usn evidence from an image to infer when they appeared.

Next (trusted offline live USB — imaging step; required to perform proper timeline)
Do this from a known-clean machine or boot a trusted live USB on the target (offline). The core idea: make a forensically sound image, then examine the image with forensic tools.

A. Prepare and image (minimal, exact commands)
- Boot clean Ubuntu live USB offline. Plug in an external drive with enough free space for the image.
- Identify devices:
  sudo lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,LABEL,MODEL
  sudo fdisk -l

- Create an image with ddrescue (recommended over dd for reliability):
  sudo apt update && sudo apt install gddrescue sleuthkit libevtx-tools -y
  sudo mkdir /mnt/external
  sudo mount /dev/sdX1 /mnt/external   # replace sdX1 with your external drive partition
  sudo ddrescue -v /dev/sdY /mnt/external/target_disk.img /mnt/external/target_disk.log
  - /dev/sdY = the target drive (the one with hd1,msdos1)
  - /mnt/external = your external storage mount point

B. Locate the NTFS partition offset (if using raw image):
  sudo mmls /mnt/external/target_disk.img

C. Use SleuthKit to find the file and MFT inode, then get metadata
  # find file names in image (recursive)
  sudo fls -r -o <offset> /mnt/external/target_disk.img | grep -i OfflineRecEnvTrace

  # fls output gives inode numbers; then get detailed metadata (istat)
  sudo istat -o <offset> /mnt/external/target_disk.img <inode>

  istat output shows NTFS MFT timestamps:
  - mtime (modified)
  - atime (access)
  - ctime (entry changed)
  - crtime / birth (creation) — when available from MFT

D. Examine USN Journal and $MFT to correlate creation times
  - Use ntfs-3g/ntfsinfo or tools that can dump $MFT records. Example:
    sudo apt install ntfs-3g
    sudo ntfsinfo -f /dev/mapper/loop0p1  # (advanced: map image partitions)
  - Or use specialized tools (log2timeline/Plaso) to parse the USN Journal and produce events.

E. Parse EVTX logs for events mentioning recovery/repair
  - Extract the EVTX files (from image or mounted partition):
    cp /mnt/evidence/Windows/System32/winevt/Logs/System.evtx /mnt/external/
  - Parse with evtx_dump / wevtx:
    evtx_dump /mnt/external/System.evtx | less
  - Search for events around the time stamps that mention “startup repair”, “offline recovery”, file writes, or mounting events.

How to interpret results
- If MFT/crtime/USN indicate the file was created after your earlier inspection timestamp, that is evidence the file appeared later — consistent with an offline actor altering the disk between your two observations.
- If timestamps predate your first inspection or match known Windows recovery times, that suggests the files were present earlier (and may be benign Windows recovery artifacts).
- Cross-correlate: MFT birth time + USN Journal entry + EVTX events = stronger timeline.

If you cannot image now and need an interim measure (only to document current state)
- From grub, capture:
  - Exact ls outputs (as above).
  - Check for presence in other likely folders:
    ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/Recovery/
    ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/System32/LogFiles/SRT/
    ls (hd1,msdos1)/Windows/Panther/
- Photograph the screen (timestamped on your phone) and keep the phone off network.

Notes & caveats (important)
- GRUB listing outputs are useful as contemporaneous evidence but are weaker than forensic analysis of an image.
- File timestamps can be forged but MFT/USN + EVTX correlation makes forging harder; still, consider the whole evidence set.
- Preserve chain-of-custody: label drives, log times, keep external drives offline after imaging.

## Copilot

Acknowledged — top option. Minimal, exact plan to create a UEFI rescue USB on a clean machine, boot target in UEFI mode, then use that environment to reliably execute (or inspect) the on-disk EFI loader. No extra commentary.

Prereqs (must be done on a clean machine)
- Trusted, clean machine to write the USB.
- Ubuntu Desktop ISO (or other UEFI-capable live ISO) and its SHA256 verified locally.
- A USB drive to burn the ISO (all data on USB will be destroyed).

Step 1 — Create UEFI rescue USB (on clean machine)
- Verify ISO checksum, then write the ISO to the USB with dd (replace /path/to/ubuntu.iso and /dev/sdX with actual values).

```bash name=create-uefi-rescue-usb.sh
# Verify SHA256 (example)
sha256sum /path/to/ubuntu-xx.iso

# Write ISO to USB (replace /dev/sdX with the USB device, NOT a partition like /dev/sdX1)
sudo dd if=/path/to/ubuntu-xx.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress conv=fsync && sync
```

Step 2 — Boot the target from the USB in UEFI mode
- Enter firmware setup -> Boot -> ensure UEFI mode enabled and USB as UEFI boot; disable legacy/CSM if needed.
- Boot the USB and choose "Try Ubuntu" (offline).

Short answer: sudo is just the tool to run privileged commands. On an Ubuntu live‑USB the default live user typically has passwordless sudo enabled, so you can run administrative commands without a password. If you’re already in a root shell you don’t need sudo.

sudo lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,PARTLABEL,MOUNTPOINT
sudo fdisk -l

If sudo prompts for a password or is unavailable, try:
sudo -v
(if that fails and you can’t become root, reboot the machine and recreate the live USB with a known Ubuntu desktop image — the desktop live image provides passwordless sudo by default.)

One‑line risk: using sudo runs commands as root — only run the lsblk/fdisk checks (read‑only); avoid write actions until you confirm.
